title: Using seaweed in compost
subtitle: Seaweed as a nitrogen activator and micronutrient source in a home compost heap
excerpt: Fresh seaweed is a useful compost activator: nitrogen-rich (roughly 1.5 percent N), high in potassium and trace minerals, and fast-decomposing. A thin layer of seaweed between carbon layers accelerates the heat phase in a poorly-activated heap. This guide covers how much to add, how to handle the salt content, and the practical limits of using beach seaweed legally.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic seaweed as compost activator guidance. RHS guidance on seaweed in the garden. Marine Conservation Society position on seaweed collection.

glossaryTerms.definition: The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of composting material. Browns (high carbon) and greens (high nitrogen) should be balanced at roughly 25:1 to 30:1 for active hot composting.
A managed composting process that maintains temperatures of 55 to 70 degrees Celsius through active turning and balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, decomposing material in 4 to 12 weeks.

content.text: Seaweed is a traditional garden amendment in coastal parts of Britain and Ireland. It is used as a soil mulch or layered into compost heaps as an activator. Kelp, bladderwrack, and rockweed are the most common species on UK beaches. All work as compost activators, with nitrogen content around 1 to 2 percent by dry weight, similar to fresh grass clippings, and a
of around 10:1 to 20:1. Added in thin layers between carbon materials, seaweed provides the nitrogen kick that starts

Fresh seaweed collected from the beach contains 1 to 3 percent sodium chloride by fresh weight. Applied directly to the soil at high rates, that salt would damage plant roots and soil microbes. In the compost heap, the salt dilutes through the whole heap volume and leaches with rain over the composting period, so by the time the compost is finished the salt level is negligible. The practical safe limit is one part seaweed (by volume) to ten parts other compost material. A 50-litre bucket of fresh seaweed added to a 500-litre heap once per season is within that limit and causes no salt problem.

Collecting beached seaweed for personal use is generally allowed under common law in England and Wales. Check local bylaws before collecting. Beached seaweed means already detached from rocks and lying above the tide line. Cutting living seaweed from rocks requires a licence in some areas. If you are unsure, contact the relevant Marine Management Organisation (MMO) district office for the area of coastline. The Marine Conservation Society recommends collecting only loose-lying seaweed in small quantities, avoiding removal of seaweed from rocky shore ecosystems.
Rinse the seaweed briefly with tap water to reduce surface salt if the seaweed was collected after a storm surge (which concentrates salt). No need to rinse if collected under normal conditions.

Layer the seaweed in 50 to 75 mm thick layers between layers of cardboard or straw. Seaweed sticks together and forms impermeable sheets if added in thick blocks; thin layers break down faster and aerate better.
Seaweed decomposes rapidly and will have largely disappeared within 2 to 4 weeks in an active heap. Unlike woody material, it requires no shredding.

Dried seaweed meal provides the same mineral benefits without the salt or collection logistics. It is sold in garden centres as a soil improver. Apply at the rate on the packet. Liquid seaweed extract can be diluted and watered onto a slow or dry heap to boost nitrogen and microbial activity. Use at 10 times the foliar-feed dilution and water in evenly.

attrs.symptom: Seaweed forming a slimy mat in the heap

content.text: The layers were too thick or too wet. Fork through the slimy zone to break it up and add cardboard to restore airflow.

attrs.symptom: Strong fishy smell after adding seaweed

content.text: Normal for fresh seaweed in the first day or two. Cover immediately with a thick layer of cardboard. The smell dissipates as the seaweed starts to decompose.
